devotionals.title: Refreshing Rest
devotionals.body:
Salvation is the primary, precious, grace-gift from our loving God to us. It is not an inactive item in our life. It is not an award we put up as decoration and remember only when we pass by its display case. Salvation is an explosion of grace — the ignition to the Christian walk. It comes with innumerable gifts all immense, with salvation itself being a gift of immeasurable value. This gift comes with the promises of forgiveness, justification, eternal life, hope, and glorification. Therein, the organized beauty of God's web of promises, lies a simple point: a refreshing drop of dew atop the expansive interconnection of favor — God's gift of rest to His redeemed.

Jesus calls two groups of people into the bosom of His eternal rest. First called are the weary: those who tirelessly labor daily, parsing their lives and ambitions in search of purpose. They foresee their futures; they envision their younger years spent in constant toil, earning, yearning, and fighting to survive. They see the culmination of their lives as a tightrope of sacrifice needed to balance their desires and relationships, lest they plummet to failure. Still, in all this, they are emptied by a hope that must find constant replacement. As they near the end of their strenuous path, when frailty keeps them remembering, they ask, "What was it all for?"

Christ calls to such a man. He urges you who are wearied and fatigued would turn to Him, trusting in the love displayed in His death on the cross and the surety of His resurrection, so we may stand victorious over sin. He is calling you to make Him your fulfilling purpose.

Second, Jesus calls to those who are heavy-laden. The burdened heart carries with it a multilayered world of responsibility. Overloaded with expectations and requirements, it attempts to bear the overwhelming, crushing weight of its own standards, constantly close to crumbling and vanishing by a slip of failure. In the passage's context, these are those who believe that their work and efforts in life will save them. They hope their constant attempts to balance out their daily errors will bring them peace. Yet, they fail daily and try to mend themselves from these errors. Still, Christ calls them to Himself: those who fail and cannot fathom how to save themselves, so that He may take their burden, their failures and sins, and give them the rest of their Savior.

Where is your heart resting? If you notice that your heart has been lying far from Christ and His promise, then quickly go to Him. Stop the wearisome insanity of searching for purpose: unceasingly and wearily working to outdo your wrongs with good acts, hoping that by them and through them you will be saved and that by your toil your guilt will be quieted. All these efforts assure no rest and no answer. Only faith in Christ can give you victory over all — over sin and death, over hopelessness and despair, and onto life of eternal value and rest.

"Gentle and humble in heart." This is the One to whom you are coming: the Lord who is tenderhearted towards you. He has seen and knows your sin, understands the depth of it, and knows what is behind your heart and how it wholly purposes against His law and standard. Even so, He welcomes you to Himself, with His arms just as open, so that you may rest in His love and care and rejoice in His forgiveness.

To a soul that has found rest, Christ becomes the precious Lord of his blood-bought heart. Consider, then, your life in His hands. Is working a good service, undertaken with full effort, tiresome? If your waking hours are overwhelmed by the sum of grievous toil, either in your job or in service to the church, then notice the weight that Christ has carried away from your struggling shoulders and thus find the strength to act in love. A heart focused on love desires to push against its limits; it latches onto ambition to do it all for others, and does not let up. Love God, love people, and love the church. Love greatly and ambitiously at all times. While the mind may ache and daze into sleep, and the stomach growl and beg for substance, a loving heart is ever eager to bear God's fruit, since its soul is eternally rested and alive. The call is to "not lose heart in doing good" (Gal 6:9), and, certainly, love "bears all things ... endures all things" (1 Cor 13:7). Through Christ, God has given us His rest and His love, so our life may be fulfilled in the joy of His work as we walk this world. It is God who restores, supplies, and maintains our faith; truly, our walk in Christ is one of glad rest.

Consider:

  • Do you find yourself refreshed by Christ and His love? What wrong ideas of rest keep you from loving ambitiously?
  • Find verses that bring to mind God's promises, and reflect on the rest and security they bring.

devotionals.title: A Satisfying Purpose
devotionals.body:
Can man, by diligent search, happen upon a satisfying meaning for himself? In his grandiose schemes to try to fill the void of purposelessness, what does man make his aim? Perhaps he aims to make his locality vibrant, tidy, and peaceful, and also live a long, prosperous life. He hopes to teach his children how to make the most — or waste the least — of this one life of opportunity, and eventually have their future progeny learn to survive and find their own developed, mysterious purpose on this earth. However, the focuses and aims that belong to the world are limited and transient, fickle at uncertainty — if the sun shines too sharply upon man's plans, they'll melt and leave him destitute. How can a purpose that will not remain truly be satisfying?

God's truth makes clear the purpose of all that was made, giving a reason to creation: "For from Him and through Him and to Him are all things. To Him be the glory forever. Amen" (Romans 11:36). Thus, it is plainly shown that our purpose in creation lies beyond temporary margins and past the scope of our present existence itself — it is to uplift our lives for the glory of God. But how are we to bring Him glory, specifically?

Our mode of worship is different from that of all creation; we have values and senses that the plant and animal kingdoms do not possess. "Let us make man in Our Image, according to Our likeness..." (Genesis 1:26) — in the creation account, the making of man stands out as uniquely personal. We were not made equally to the rest of what was made: God made us in His likeness, with morality, creativity, spirituality, and personality. We are meant to reflect the God who made us to be like Him, but because of Adam's sin and disobedience, all of us are blighted by importunate sinfulness.

And so humanity has relinquished its fellowship with God in favor of its misconstrued aims, allowing its wickedness to make them "fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23). Man in his current state does not reflect the perfect glory of his Creator, stained in our divine reflection by the fall of Adam. However, God gave us a New Adam, who according to His word "is the radiance of His glory and the exact representation of His nature" (Hebrews 1:3). This New Adam is Jesus: God made flesh, His grace and truth made visible.

And so Jesus fulfills His purpose as the image of the eternal God: on earth, He was a Man whose singular desire was to do the will of His Father (John 4:34). He kept God's commands and precepts, remaining obedient and humble before His Sender. Through Him, we have salvation from the wages of sin; more than that, we are called according to God's magnificent purpose (Romans 8:28). The verse that follows this truth states, "For those whom He foreknew, He also predestined to become conformed to the image of His Son..." (Romans 8:29). Clearly, our lives have a meaning and a calling: that calling has an end goal and a faithful Lord over its accomplishing who has called us into it. Our everyday purpose is to be made nearer to the perfect image of Christ, who is the radiant image of the Father in heaven.

Daily life is a race of endurance and eagerness; by the Spirit who indwells and beckons us upward, we are formed to be less like the flesh which in Christ was crucified and reflect more to the new, perfect creation we now are. Paul writes further in Romans 8: "and those whom He predestined, He also called; and these whom He called, He also justified; and these whom He justified, He also glorified" (v. 30). God's present work in us includes the ongoing perfecting of all His saints as vessels of His grace. Vessels that are being filled as the images and testaments of Christ, and in so doing, making us a worthy reflection of God as we have been made to be since the beginning, before the entrance of sin into the landscape of creation.

Consider:

  • Where would you consider yourself not fulfilling your God-called purpose? How do you think your life can glorify God in that area instead?
  • Meditate on the moral perfection of Christ, and seek to desire the character which He calls us to have, as Him Himself does.
